answers.question: List of scenarios
answers.evidence: "In 2024, we elected to conduct a deep dive on the qualitative scenario analysis of different scenarios from the International Energy Agency (IEA): • Net Zero emissions by 2050 (NZE) … • Announced Pledges Scenario (APS) … To assess the physical impacts of climate change, we conducted an assessment for the medium term (2030) using two scenarios from the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC): SSP1 – 2.6 and SSP3 – 7.0. … Both the High and Moderate scenarios anticipate that by 2050, carbon pricing will reach levels necessary to limit global warming to within 2°C above pre-industrial levels, though the Moderate scenario assumes delayed action in the short term. The Low scenario, while not aligned with the 2°C target, is based on the implementation of Nationally Determined Contributions."
answers.location_hint: Strategy section, p.10–11; Metrics & Targets section, p.18
answers.notes: The document also references "Orderly (1.5°C)", "Too Little Too Late (>2°C)" and "Hothouse (>3°C)" in Figure 4 (p.9), which appear to be the 2023 FSC scenarios still referenced in the risk/opportunity table. The IEA NZE and APS are the primary 2024 qualitative scenarios; IPCC SSP1-2.6 and SSP3-7.0 are used for physical risk; and High/Moderate/Low are used for carbon pricing/UCC transitional risk assessment.

answers.question: Global temperature outcome by 2100
answers.answer: 1.5°C (NZE/Orderly), 1.7°C (APS), >2°C (Too Little Too Late / High and Moderate carbon pricing scenarios), 3.0°C or greater (SSP3-7.0 / Hothouse)
answers.evidence: "Net Zero emissions by 2050 (NZE) … It is consistent with limiting global temperature rise to 1.5°C. • Announced Pledges Scenario (APS) … It is consistent with limiting global temperature rise to 1.7°C. … These scenarios were analysed against a 1.5°C scenario, and a 3.0°C or greater scenario. … Both the High and Moderate scenarios anticipate that by 2050, carbon pricing will reach levels necessary to limit global warming to within 2°C above pre-industrial levels."
answers.location_hint: Strategy section p.10; Metrics & Targets p.18
answers.notes: The document references 1.5°C, 1.7°C, >2°C, and 3.0°C or greater as temperature outcomes. No explicit 2100 endpoint is stated for all scenarios, but these are the temperature targets associated with each scenario.

answers.question: Scenario provider
answers.answer: Multiple providers are explicitly named: International Energy Agency (IEA) for the NZE and APS qualitative scenarios; Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) for the SSP1-2.6 and SSP3-7.0 physical risk scenarios; OECD and IEA for the carbon pricing (UCC) scenarios. The Financial Services Council (FSC) selected the 2023 scenarios (referenced but not used in 2024).
answers.evidence: "In 2024, we elected to conduct a deep dive on the qualitative scenario analysis of different scenarios from the International Energy Agency (IEA) … To assess the physical impacts of climate change, we conducted an assessment for the medium term (2030) using two scenarios from the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC): SSP1 – 2.6 and SSP3 – 7.0. … These scenarios are informed by research from the OECD and IEA."
answers.location_hint: Strategy section p.10; Metrics & Targets p.18

answers.question: Pick-and-mix of SSPs and RCPs
answers.evidence: "To assess the physical impacts of climate change, we conducted an assessment for the medium term (2030) using two scenarios from the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC): SSP1 – 2.6 and SSP3 – 7.0."
answers.notes: The document uses SSP identifiers only (SSP1-2.6 and SSP3-7.0). No RCP identifiers are mentioned anywhere in the document. There is no mixing of SSP and RCP scenario families.

answers.question: Customised scenarios
answers.evidence: "In 2024, we elected to conduct a deep dive on the qualitative scenario analysis of different scenarios from the International Energy Agency (IEA) … We chose the IEA scenarios because the qualitative narrative available is much more extensive at a sector level."
answers.notes: The document does not use language such as "downscaled", "adapted", "localised", "customised", or "tailored". The scenarios are used off-the-shelf from the IEA and IPCC. The only adaptation described is applying the scenarios to their investment portfolio sectors.

answers.question: Co-produced climate scenarios
answers.evidence: "In 2024, we elected to conduct a deep dive on the qualitative scenario analysis of different scenarios from the International Energy Agency (IEA)"
answers.notes: No language relating to "participatory", "co-creat", "deliberat", "co-design", "stakeholder input", or "collaborative development" of scenarios is present in the document. Scenarios are sourced directly from IEA and IPCC.

answers.question: Baseline scenarios
answers.answer: Yes — a baseline is referenced in the physical risk assessment. Financial Impacts are described as "projected financial consequences of changing climate hazard exposure relative to a baseline". Additionally, for GHG emissions reporting, a baseline year of 2022 is used for comparison.
answers.evidence: "Financial Impacts – which represent the projected financial consequences of changing climate hazard exposure relative to a baseline, expressed as potential climate-related losses such as those from capital expenditure, operational expenditure or business interruptions."
answers.notes: The document references a "baseline" in the physical risk financial impact calculation, but does not describe a named baseline climate scenario (e.g. a "no-change" or "current policies" scenario) against which the climate scenarios are formally compared. The baseline appears to be a point-in-time reference for financial impact calculation rather than a distinct climate scenario.

answers.question: Model used
answer: S&P TruCost (investment ESG and climate risk platform)
answers.evidence: "Implementing S&P TruCost as our investment ESG and climate risk platform, providing us with improved access to data needed to inform future strategies." and "RLA is currently utilising S&P's CapIQ Pro platform. The underlying proprietary ESG security data is provided and collated by S&P and aggregated/calculated applying worldwide ESG standards for reporting."
answers.location_hint: Strategy section p.8; Metrics & Targets p.18 (footnote)
answers.notes: No formal climate models (e.g. GCMs, IAMs such as REMIND, MESSAGE, GCAM) or macroeconomic models (e.g. NiGEM, DICE) are named. The document references S&P TruCost and S&P CapIQ Pro as data/analysis platforms. An unnamed "independent provider of climate-related market analysis" is also referenced for the carbon pricing dataset.

answers.question: Damage from acute physical risk considered
answers.evidence: "The physical risk assessment framework considers eight major climate hazards: wildfires, extreme cold, extreme heat, water stress, coastal flood, river line flood, tropical cyclone and drought. The assessment produces two key outputs: Exposure Scores – which provide a point-in-time measure of exposure to climate hazards – and Financial Impacts – which represent the projected financial consequences of changing climate hazard exposure relative to a baseline, expressed as potential climate-related losses such as those from capital expenditure, operational expenditure or business interruptions."
answers.notes: The document explicitly includes acute physical hazards (wildfires, floods, tropical cyclones, extreme heat/cold) and quantifies financial impacts (as % of portfolio). Both acute and chronic hazards (water stress, drought) are included in the framework.

answers.question: Non-temperature variables in scenarios
answers.evidence: "Carbon pricing is one of the key transition risks … Central to this assessment is the quantification of an Unpriced Carbon Cost (UCC) … The physical risk assessment framework considers eight major climate hazards: wildfires, extreme cold, extreme heat, water stress, coastal flood, river line flood, tropical cyclone and drought. … Climate risks and opportunities were identified at a sector level at different time horizons across various assessment lenses, including Policy, Technology, Reputation and Market."

answers.question: Scale of scenarios
answers.evidence: "In 2024, we elected to conduct a deep dive on the qualitative scenario analysis of different scenarios from the International Energy Agency (IEA): • Net Zero emissions by 2050 (NZE) – shows a narrow but achievable path for the global energy sector to achieve net zero CO2 emissions by 2050 … To assess the physical impacts of climate change, we conducted an assessment for the medium term (2030) using two scenarios from the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC): SSP1 – 2.6 and SSP3 – 7.0."
answers.location_hint: Strategy section p.10; Metrics & Targets p.18
answers.notes: The document does not mention any downscaling to regional or local level. The IEA scenarios are global energy sector scenarios, and the IPCC SSP scenarios are global. The UCC carbon pricing dataset varies by "geographic regions where emissions occur" but this is not described as a downscaled climate scenario.

answers.question: Time frames
answers.answer: Short-term: 0–3 years (out to 2025); Medium-term: 3–10 years (out to 2030); Long-term: 10+ years (out to 2050+). No explicit rationale for these specific timeframes is provided beyond their use in the scenario analysis framework.
answers.evidence: "Our Strategy section also outlines our time horizons to assess the potential impacts of climate-related risks which include short-term (0-3 years), medium-term (3-10 years), long-term (10+ years). … Short-term (out to 2025) … Medium-term (out to 2030) … Long-term (out to 2050+)"
answers.notes: The document states the time horizons but does not provide an explicit justification for why these specific periods were chosen (e.g. no reference to asset lifetimes, policy cycles, or regulatory requirements as the basis for the timeframe selection).

answers.question: Justification for scenario/model choice
answers.evidence: "We chose the IEA scenarios because the qualitative narrative available is much more extensive at a sector level. This has allowed us to go into granular detail for each sector which we otherwise may not have been able to do using other scenarios. We have also aligned our scenario analysis to RLGH, who have also used the IEA scenarios."
answers.notes: Justification is provided for the IEA scenario choice (sector-level narrative depth and group alignment). No explicit justification is provided for the choice of IPCC SSP1-2.6 and SSP3-7.0 for physical risk, or for the High/Moderate/Low carbon pricing scenarios.
